description:
A Sales Order is the central business document that formalizes a customer's purchase commitment and orchestrates end-to-end order fulfillment. From a business perspective, it serves as the single source of truth that connects sales, items ordered, operations, finance, and customer service teams around a unified transaction.

It serves four main functions:
1. Financial & Customer Agreement: Captures billing/shipping details, pricing, discounts, taxes, payment terms, expected profits, and salesperson commissions to formalize the commercial contract.
2. Product Configuration: Details the exact specifications of ordered items—including custom dimensions, styles, materials, finishes, and option parameters—to ensure accurate production and customer satisfaction.
3. Manufacturing Driver: Links ordered items directly to their Bill of Materials (BOM), dictating raw material requirements, component hierarchies, and assembly schedules for shop floor execution.
4. Logistics & Fulfillment: Tracks the physical lifecycle from warehouse allocation, packing, and shipping weights to carrier assignment, delivery confirmation, and post-sale installation or service.

Trigger Keywords: [Sales Order, Order, SO, Customer Order, Sales Record, Order Document, Sales Transaction, Fulfillment Order, Job Order, Project Order, Order Entry, Order Header, Customer Purchase, Sales File, Order Request, SO Header, Order Master]

Additional Information: A sales order may have sub-orders or change orders, identified by the original order number followed by a hyphen and sequence number (e.g., 903930-1, 903930-2).

The hierarchical manufacturing recipe tied directly to a sales order line item. It defines the parent-child assembly structure, detailing the exact sub-components, required quantities, raw material constraints, and unit costs necessary to build the ordered product.

A financial sub-record tracking supplementary fees applied specifically to an individual line item. It captures line-level surchargesâ€”such as specialized handling, freight allocation, or custom service feesâ€”keeping them distinct from the item's base unit price.

The financial attribution record that tracks sales representative compensation for a given order. It defines exactly who is credited for the sale (Salesperson), their specific payout rate (Commission %), and how the total revenue is prorated among multiple contributors in the event of a shared deal (Split Sales %).
Percentage of the sales value allocated as commission for the salesperson.
Links the commission entry to its parent sales order.
Identifies the individual or representative earning the commission.
Sequence number of the commission record within the sales order.
Percentage of the sale assigned to a salesperson when multiple contributors are involved.

short_description:
A sales order is a record of a customer's purchase that lists the items ordered, their quantities and prices, along with billing, shipping, and payment details.
Additional Information: A sales order may have sub-orders or change orders, identified by the original order number followed by a hyphen and sequence number (e.g., 903930-1, 903930-2).

description:
A Production Order is the primary internal execution document that drives the manufacturing and assembly process of items or products on the shop floor. 
Explicit Distinction: Unlike a Sales Order, which captures the commercial transaction with the customer, the Production Order is strictly focused on the physical creation of the items. 
It serves as the master blueprint for operations, translating customer commitments into actionable labor routing, material consumption, and shop floor tasks.

It serves four main functions:
1. Unified Manufacturing Record: Consolidates all producible items from a Sales Order—including its sub-orders or change orders—into a single production run.
2. Shop Floor Routing: Defines the operational sequence, assigning tasks to work centers and tracking actual vs. planned start/finish dates.
3. Material Planning: Links items to their Bill of Materials (BOM), specifying components, raw material constraints, costs, and scrap allowances.
4. Configuration & Output Tracking: Translates customer specs into producible pieces while tracking quantities, weight, and volume for fulfillment handoff.

Trigger Keywords: [Production Order, Manufacturing Order, Work Order, Prod Order, Shop Floor Order, Job Ticket, Assembly Order, Fabrication Order, Production Record, Routing Order, Manufacturing Job, Work Ticket, Production Ticket]

The primary manufacturing execution record detailing a specific item to be built. It serves as the critical bridge between customer demand and the shop floor by explicitly linking back to its originating Sales Order Item (SALES_ORDER_ITEM_SEQ_NUM). It tracks production quantities, lot/serial assignments, physical dimensions (weight, volume), and fulfillment progress.

The execution-level configuration record. It captures the specific variants, custom parameters, or stylistic choices (e.g., custom dimensions, finishes, or colors) required by the shop floor to accurately manufacture the requested version of the item.
Links the item style parameter record to its parent production order.
Sequence number ordering this parameter within the item's configuration list.

The hierarchical manufacturing recipe dictating the exact raw materials and sub-assemblies needed to build the production item. Because this is an execution-level record, it accounts for shop-floor realities, including raw material constraints, precise unit costs, and anticipated material loss (Scrap % and Scrap Quantity).
Links the bill of materials (BOM) record to its parent production order.
Sequence number of the production order line item this BOM entry supports.
Unique sequence number identifying this specific component entry within the BOM.
Depth level of this component within the BOM hierarchy, indicating its position in the assembly tree.
References the BOM sequence number of the parent component, establishing the hierarchical structure.
The SKU or identifier of the immediate parent assembly component in the BOM.
The specific component or raw material SKU required to manufacture the parent item.
Quantity of this raw material or component required to produce the parent item.
The standard or actual financial cost per unit of this specific BOM component.
Total physical weight of the required quantity for this BOM component.
Specific manufacturing constraints governing the properties of this raw material.
Expected percentage of material loss, waste, or scrap during the production process.

The operational routing and labor tracking record. It defines the step-by-step chronological operations (Task Order) required to build the item, assigns each step to a specific machine or department (Work Center), and logs the actual start and finish timestamps to track production floor efficiency.
Links the manufacturing task to its parent production order.
Sequence number of the production order line item this task is associated with.

Identifier for the overarching routing plan or production pathway this task belongs to.
The specific routing operation, machine code, or process step to be performed.
The chronological execution order of this task relative to other tasks in the sequence.
The physical workstation, machine, or department responsible for completing this task.
The recorded date and time this task physically began on the production floor.
The recorded date and time this manufacturing task was fully completed.

short_description:
A production order is a manufacturing record that specifies what items to produce, their quantities, required materials, and production steps, linked to a customer's sales request.
Additional Information: A single production order is created per sales request, consolidating the main record and all its sub- or change variants

description:
A manifest is a logistical planning entity used to group, organize, and schedule multiple sales orders for future delivery. It serves as a planned load or dispatch document representing the orders assigned to a specific truck, route, or scheduled pickup date. Users create manifests to plan their fulfillment operations—determining which orders are shipped together, when they leave the facility, and how they are routed. A manifest acts as a logical container for multiple "manifest orders" (the underlying sales orders) and is strictly a planning and scheduling tool utilized prior to the physical execution of a shipment.

Trigger Keywords: [manifest, shipment manifest, dispatch plan, load planning, scheduled pickup, delivery schedule, truck load, route plan, grouped orders, manifest orders, dispatch schedule, pickup date]

The logistical junction record that assigns and schedules a specific Sales Order onto a master fulfillment Manifest. It acts as the line-item detail for a Manifest, defining exactly which customer orders are grouped together for a single shipment or route. It captures the subset of the order being fulfilled, tracking the specific item counts, physical dimensions (Weight, Cubes), and associated project data required for load planning.

description: The execution record detailing the physical dispatch and movement of goods out the door. You MUST map to this key if the query involves the active transport phase of the cabinetry. This includes explicit keywords like 'shipments', 'shipping', 'loaded', 'dispatched', 'delivered', 'tracking details', 'tracking numbers', or 'packing slips'. It represents the actual real-world transit of the finished cabinetry to the customer.

The logistical execution record detailing the specific physical goods being dispatched. It serves as the bridge between order intent and physical fulfillment by explicitly linking back to both the revenue side (via SALES_ORDER_NUM and SALES_ORDER_ITEM_SEQ_NUM) and the manufacturing side (via PROD_ORDER_ITEM_SEQ_NUM). It tracks the granular lifecycle of the inventory moving out the door, including exact packaging dimensions, warehouse staging locations, and the precise quantities ordered, shipped, and eventually received by the customer.

description: The definitive financial and billing document requesting payment. You MUST map to this key if the query relates to money, billing, or financial records. This includes exact or generic references to an 'invoice', 'sales invoice', 'bill', 'payment', 'deposit', 'balance due', 'revenue', or specific identifiers like 'invoice number', 'invoice ID', or formats like 'INV-1001'. It represents the fiscal culmination of an order.

The date by which payment for the invoice is expected.

The primary financial destination legally responsible for the invoice payment. It contains the BILL_TO company name, financial contact, and address details necessary for accounts receivable processing, statement generation, and payment collection.
Customer reference number for the billing account.
Company name on the billing address.
Contact person associated with the billing address.
Primary street address for billing.
Secondary street address line for billing.
City for the billing address.
State for the billing address.
Zip code for the billing address.
Country for the billing address.
Primary phone number for the billing contact.
Mobile phone number for the billing contact.
Alternate phone number for the billing contact.
Fax number for the billing contact.
Email address for the billing contact.

The recorded customer location or branch that officially originated the purchase. Preserved on the invoice, these SELL_TO details are typically used for localizing revenue reporting, verifying sales territories, or determining specific tax jurisdictions.
Contact person for the sell-to account.
Primary street address for the sell-to location.
Secondary street address line for the sell-to location.
City for the sell-to address.
State for the sell-to address.
Zip code for the sell-to address.
Country for the sell-to address.
Primary phone number for the sell-to contact.
Mobile phone number for the sell-to contact.
Alternate phone number for the sell-to contact.
Fax number for the sell-to contact.
Email address for the sell-to contact.

The verified physical destination where the goods were delivered. On the invoice, these SHIP_TO details (including specific subdivision codes) confirm the final delivery point for the billed items, which is critical for validating location-based sales taxes and freight charge calculations.
Code identifying the subdivision associated with the delivery.
Name of the subdivision associated with the shipping address.
Contact person at the shipping destination.
Primary street address for the shipment destination.
Secondary street address line for the shipment destination.
City for the shipping destination.
State for the shipping destination.
Zip code for the shipping destination.
Country for the shipping destination.
Primary phone number for the shipping contact.
Mobile phone number for the shipping contact.
Alternate phone number for the shipping contact.
Fax number for the shipping contact.
Email address for the shipping contact.

The core financial line-item record detailing the specific products or services billed to the customer. It locks in the definitive financial metricsâ€”including exact unit pricing, taxes, discounts, and calculated line amountsâ€”at the exact time of billing. It maintains strict backward traceability to the originating demand (SALES_ORDER_ITEM_SEQ_NUM) and acts as a reconciliation point for fulfillment gaps (e.g., tracking Qty Shipped Not Invoiced).
Linked sales  invoice identifier..
Sequence number of the line item on the invoice.
Linked sales order identifier.
Sequence number of the corresponding sales order line item.
Identifier of the invoiced product or service.
Customer's specific part or item number reference.
Serial number for tracked inventory items.
Base cost of a single unit of the item.
Original list price before discounts or taxes.
Final selling price per unit after adjustments.
Total line amount before tax (Qty x Unit Price).
Total line amount including applicable taxes.
Calculated commission earned on this specific line item.
Percentage discount applied to the item.
Monetary value of the discount applied to the line.
Percentage markup applied over the unit cost.
Tax classification code determining the item's tax rate.
Tax percentage rate applied to the item.
Dynamic configurable values or attributes for the item.
Total quantity invoiced for the item.
Quantity that has been shipped but is not yet billed.
Cubic volume of a single unit of the item.
Total weight of the invoiced quantity.
Warehouse storage area from which the item was fulfilled.
Specific shelf or bin location in the warehouse.

The historical configuration record capturing the exact product variants, styles, or custom parameters that were officially billed. This ensures the financial document retains a permanent, immutable record of the specific custom attributes (like dimensions or finishes) of the finalized product.
Linked sales invoice  identifier.
Sequence number of the item option parameter record.
Style variant or option selected for the item.
Name of the configurable product parameter.
Selected value for the corresponding parameter.

A localized financial sub-record detailing specific, line-level fees added to an invoiced item. It isolates supplementary costsâ€”such as specialized handling, freight allocations, or assembly surchargesâ€”from the item's base unit price, ensuring clean revenue accounting and tax calculation.
Linked sales invoice identifier.
Sequence number of the associated invoice line item.
Unique sequence number for this specific charge record.
Item identifier to which the charge is applied.
Classification of the charge (e.g., freight, handling, assembly).
Charge amount applied per individual unit.
Total monetary amount of the applied charge.
Percentage rate used to calculate the charge amount.
